---
title: "Loading Data"
description: "The three ways to get data into data.js — Data.feed, Data.object and Data.import — and every supported source type."
---

data.js has three loading entry points. They all end up producing the same thing — a `Data.Table` — but differ in where the source data comes from and whether loading is asynchronous.

## `Data.feed(options)` — load from a URL

The main entry point. Fetches from a URL and is always asynchronous:

```javascript
Data.feed({ source: szUrl, type: "csv" }).load(function (mydata) {
    // mydata is a Data.Table
}).error(function (e) {
    // handle the failure
});
```

Use this whenever your data lives at a URL — which is the common case.

## `Data.object(options)` — load from data you already have

Same idea, but `source` is a JavaScript value you already hold in memory (e.g. the body of a response you fetched yourself), not a URL. Still asynchronous — call `.import()` with a callback:

```javascript
Data.object({ source: response, type: "json" }).import(function (mydata) {
    // mydata is a Data.Table
});
```

For binary formats (`parquet`, `geoparquet`, `gpkg`, `flatgeobuf`, `geobuf`) `source` must already be an `ArrayBuffer` — `Data.object` does no fetching for these, it only parses what you hand it.

## `Data.import(options)` — synchronous shortcut

A synchronous convenience wrapper around `Data.object`:

```javascript
var table = Data.import({ source: response, type: "json" });
var ids = table.column("id").values();
```

:::{.callout-important}
## Only safe for synchronous parsers
`Data.import()` returns immediately — there's no callback, no `.load()`/`.import()` to wait on. It works for parsers that run synchronously (json, csv, geojson, and similar), but returns `undefined` for anything genuinely asynchronous (e.g. `jsonstat`, which lazy-loads its own parser script). If you're not sure, use `Data.object(...).import(callback)` instead.
:::

## Supported source types

Pass one of these as `type` to any of the three loaders above. See [Source Types](source_types.qmd) for the full options reference (the `parquet` row in particular has several bbox-mode-only options covered in [Remote Parquet by Bounding Box](remote_parquet_bbox.qmd)).

| `type` | What it loads |
|---|---|
| `"csv"` | Plain-text CSV (delimiters `,` and `;`), parsed with Papa Parse |
| `"json"` | A JSON object |
| `"geojson"` | A GeoJSON object |
| `"topojson"` | TopoJSON |
| `"kml"` | Keyhole Markup Language |
| `"gml"` | Geography Markup Language |
| `"jsonl"` / `"ndjson"` | Newline-delimited JSON |
| `"jsonstat"` | JSON-stat formatted object |
| `"rss"` | XML RSS feed |
| `"parquet"` | Parquet file, via DuckDB WASM. With `bbox` set, queries a remote URL directly instead of downloading it — see [Remote Parquet by Bounding Box](remote_parquet_bbox.qmd) |
| `"geoparquet"` | GeoParquet file, via DuckDB WASM, converted to GeoJSON |
| `"gpkg"` | GeoPackage file, via DuckDB WASM's spatial extension |
| `"flatgeobuf"` / `"fgb"` | FlatGeobuf binary geospatial format |
| `"geobuf"` / `"pbf"` | Geobuf (Protocol Buffer) geospatial format |
| `"jsonDB"` | data.js's own internal table format (used internally, e.g. when passing an already-built `Data.Table`) |

Type names are case-insensitive variants of the above (`CSV`, `GeoJSON`, `TOPOJSON`, etc. all work).

:::{.callout-note}
## Omitting `type`
For a plain URL, data.js can auto-detect the type from the file extension for a few formats (`.jsonl`/`.ndjson`). For everything else, always pass `type` explicitly.
:::

---

Next: [Transforming Tables](transforming_tables.qmd) to filter, pivot and reshape whatever you just loaded.
